confidence.label: Partial live data
confidence.text: Live dashboard currently uses reliable GHL conversation ownership/unread pressure plus recent Retell call activity. It is manager-useful now, but not yet full rep scorecard coverage.

managerActions: Pull the top unread inbound queues first. Those are the cleanest manager interventions.
Coach the rep with the highest stale conversation count before pushing more new leads.
Use the Retell feed to spot recent no-connect / voicemail patterns and tighten callback handling.
Treat unanswered inbound conversations as the priority leak before more reporting vanity work.

recentQueue.lastMessageBody:
Fyi


---------- Forwarded message ---------
From: Zachary Cherry <zcherry@cherrycapllc.com [zcherry@cherrycapllc.com]>
Date: Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 6:08 PM
Subject: Re: Demand for Refund of $15,000 Retainer – 7875 Oscar Green Rd Transaction
To: Lee Lindsey <leelindsey7@gmail.com [leelindsey7@gmail.com]>
CC: Andrew DeMaio <ad@amd2capital.com [ad@amd2capital.com]>



Received. We will formally respond by close of business Monday.


Sincerely,


ZC


image.png
Zachary A. Cherry, Managing Partner
Cherry Capital LLC, 917.952.9877(m)





On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 8:45 AM Lee Lindsey <leelindsey7@gmail.com [leelindsey7@gmail.com]> wrote:


> Subject: Demand for Refund of $15,000 Retainer – 7875 Oscar Green Rd Transaction
> 
>  
> 
> Zachary, Andrew, and Cherry Capital Team,
> 
>  
> 
> I am writing to formally demand the immediate return of the $15,000 retainer I paid in connection with the financing of 7875
> Oscar Green Rd.
> 
>  
> 
> This demand is based on your failure to deliver a fundable loan under the terms and representations made at the outset of our
> engagement.
> 
>  
> 
> From our initial conversations through issuance of your term sheet, the deal was presented to me as viable based on the full
> transparency I provided regarding my credit profile, liquidity, experience, and the complete scope of the project. None of these
> factors changed at any point during the process. The property, purchase price, valuation assumptions, and scope of work also
> remained consistent throughout.
> 
>  
> 
> As you know, changes to loan terms are typically driven by changes in borrower profile or deal fundamentals. Neither occurred
> here.
> 
>  
> 
> Despite this, the loan ultimately presented to me was materially different from what was originally offered, including:
> 
> - A different lender than what was represented during the process
> 
> - A fundamentally different structure, including lockbox and draw-based controls rather than the lump-sum funding that was
> confirmed to me
> 
> - Reduced and reconfigured proceeds that do not align with the original deal framework
> 
>  
> 
> Throughout the process, I was repeatedly told that we were on track to close, and those assurances were communicated not only to
> me but also to my realtor, the seller’s agent, the title company, and the insurance provider. At the same time, essential items
> required for closing were never provided, including lender identification, funding account details required for FinCEN
> compliance, loan number, and mortgage clause, despite multiple requests from third parties.
> 
>  
> 
> In addition, I was explicitly told that the loan was coming from a “family office private lender.” The subsequent presentation
> of an institutional lender with materially different requirements further confirms that the originally represented capital
> source was not in place or not capable of executing the deal as structured.
> 
>  
> 
> Further, I was specifically led to believe that the lender ultimately presented (Unitas Funding) would be able to fund the full
> rehab budget at closing in a lump sum, which was a critical requirement of this transaction and was expressly confirmed to me
> during the process. In reality, the lender’s structure requires controlled disbursements, including lockbox and draw-based
> funding, and does not provide full upfront funding of rehab proceeds. This represents a material misstatement of the lender’s
> capabilities and further demonstrates that the terms presented to me were not aligned with the actual product being offered.
> 
>  
> 
> Equally important, on March 12 I was informed that final loan documents would be circulated that same day and that we were
> proceeding toward closing, while at the same time a new lender (Unitas Funding) was being introduced for the first time. That
> lender had not completed due diligence, had not conducted an appraisal, and would not have been in a position to fund within
> that timeframe. Upon information and belief, the lender would have required a minimum of several weeks to complete underwriting
> and approval. This confirms that the representations of an imminent closing at that stage were not achievable and were
> inconsistent with the actual status of the transaction.
> 
>  
> 
> The final term sheet presented on the eve of closing bore little resemblance to what we had agreed upon and was not workable for
> my project.
> 
>  
> 
> To be clear, the sequence of events reflects a consistent pattern:
> 
> - Feb 20–21: Retainer was paid following confirmation that the transaction was “good to go” and moving forward
> 
> - Feb 25–27: Repeated requests for basic lender information (loan number, mortgage clause, funding details) were not fulfilled
> 
> - Feb 27: Loan described as coming from a “family office private lender,” without specific or verifiable lender identification
> 
> - Mar 2–10: Closing continued to be scheduled while required information for insurance, title, and FinCEN compliance remained
> outstanding
> 
> - Mar 11: I was told the transaction was “on track to sign and fund Friday”
> 
> - Mar 12: I was told final documents would be circulated “midday,” and multiple parties were led to believe closing would occur
> as scheduled
> 
> - Mar 12 (same day): A new term sheet with a different lender and materially different terms was introduced and requested to be
> signed immediately
> 
> - Post-closing: The only structure available required a lockbox and draw-based funding, directly contradicting prior
> confirmations and rendering the deal unworkable
> 
>  
> 
> At no point during this timeline were the fundamental borrower qualifications or deal parameters altered. The changes in terms
> were therefore not driven by risk or underwriting factors, but by the inability to execute the originally presented loan.
> 
>  
> 
> I paid the $15,000 retainer based on the understanding that you had a viable lending solution aligned with my clearly defined
> parameters. That was not the case. No loan was funded under the agreed structure, and the alternative presented was materially
> different and not viable.
> 
>  
> 
> Per the terms of our agreement, the retainer is refundable in the event that the lender does not fund. That condition has been
> met.
> 
>  
> 
> Accordingly, I expect the $15,000 retainer to be returned in full.
> 
>  
> 
> Please confirm in writing within five (5) business days that the refund will be processed, along with the timing of payment.
> 
>  
> 
> If this matter is not resolved promptly, I will pursue all available remedies.
> 
>  
> 
> Lee Lindsey
> 
>  
> 
>  
> 
>

Hi Deric,

A new lead has been assigned to Jayson. Please see the details below:

Name: Greyson Bragg

Email: gbragg5@yahoo.com

Phone: (720) 483-1779
Sorry for calling you multiple times my Bluetooth was acting up on me.
Mark thanks for your help on this. That is a big swing in the ARV. Did the other lender you reached out to have an ARV that they came up with?

When you get the chance, how is lending in Virginia? We  want to move to a place where the winter will not affect the rehab and selling potential once the next season comes around. Thank you for everything your doing to make this deal worth it.
